title: Food Culture & Eating Etiquette
content: Food is the heart of Lebanese culture. Meals are communal, generous, and deeply social. Understanding food etiquette is essential to navigating Lebanese life — refusing food too quickly is considered rude, and the host will always insist multiple times.

items.label: تفضّل / تفضّلي (tfaḍḍal / tfaḍḍali)
items.value: The universal invitation to eat, sit, or come in. You will hear this dozens of times at any Lebanese gathering.

items.label: صحتين (ṣaḥtēn)
items.value: Literally 'two healths' — said before or during a meal, like 'bon appétit'. Response: عَ قلبك (ʕa ʔalbak) — 'on your heart'.

items.label: The three refusals
items.value: When offered food, you must refuse at least 2-3 times before accepting. The host will insist. Accepting too quickly seems greedy; refusing permanently offends.

items.label: Mezze culture
items.value: Lebanese meals start with مازة (māzza) — 20+ small shared dishes. Everyone eats from the same plates. Ordering individual dishes is uncommon in traditional settings.

items.label: يِسلَمو إيديك (yislamu īdēk)
items.value: Literally 'may your hands be safe' — said to whoever prepared the food. One of the most important compliments you can give.

title: Service Taxi Etiquette
content: The سرفيس (servīs) is Lebanon's unofficial public transit — shared taxis that run fixed routes for a fixed fare. Understanding how they work is essential for getting around.

items.label: Hailing a service
items.value: Stand on the roadside, point in your direction. The driver slows down, you shout your destination. If they're going that way, they nod or say تفضل.

items.label: Fixed fare vs taxi
items.value: Service = shared ride, fixed low fare. Taxi = private ride, negotiate price BEFORE getting in. If you say 'taxi' the price jumps 5x.

items.label: عالحمرا (ʕa-l-Ḥamra) / عالأشرفية (ʕa-l-Ashrafiyye)
items.value: You call out your destination as a neighborhood, not an exact address. The driver drops you on the main road of that area.

items.label: Sharing with strangers
items.value: A service carries up to 4-5 passengers going roughly the same direction. You sit with strangers. Conversations sometimes happen, sometimes not.

items.label: عَ هالزاوية لو سمحت (ʕa ha-z-zāwye law samaḥt)
items.value: At this corner, please — how you tell the driver where to stop. There are no official stops.

title: Lebanese Hospitality Rules
content: Lebanese hospitality (كَرم / karam) is legendary and non-negotiable. When visiting a Lebanese home, there are unwritten rules that every guest should know. Breaking these rules won't get you in trouble, but following them earns deep respect.

items.label: Never arrive empty-handed
items.value: Bring sweets, fruit, or flowers when visiting. Even a box of حلويات (ḥalawiyyāt / pastries) from a local shop shows respect.

items.label: Shoes off at the door
items.value: Most Lebanese homes expect you to remove shoes. The host may say ما في داعي (mā fī dāʕi / no need), but do it anyway.

items.label: القهوة العربية (il-ʔahwe il-ʕarabiyye)
items.value: You will be offered Arabic coffee. Accept the first cup. The host won't stop offering until you wiggle the cup side to side — the signal you're done.

items.label: نوّرتو البيت (nawwarto il-bēt)
items.value: You lit up the house — the host says this to welcome you. Response: البيت بيتكم (il-bēt bētkum) — the house is yours.

items.label: Staying too short is rude
items.value: A quick visit is seen as dismissive. Plan to spend at least an hour, ideally more. Leaving immediately after eating is considered insulting.

title: Arabizi — Arabic in Latin Letters
content: Lebanese youth text in 'Arabizi' — Arabic written with Latin letters and numbers replacing sounds that don't exist in English. This is the de facto writing system for informal digital communication in Lebanon.

items.label: 2 = ء (hamza/glottal stop)
items.value: 2ahwe = قهوة (coffee), 2al = قال (he said)

items.label: 3 = ع (ʕayn)
items.value: 3arabi = عربي (Arabic), 3ala = على (on)

items.label: 7 = ح (ḥā)
items.value: 7abibi = حبيبي (my love), 7ilu = حلو (nice)

items.label: 5 = خ (khā)
items.value: 5alas = خلص (done), 5od = خود (take)

items.label: 8 = غ (ghayn) or ق (qāf)
items.value: 8ali = غالي (expensive), though 2 is more common for ق

items.label: Why it exists
items.value: Pre-smartphone keyboards had no Arabic letters. The system stuck. Now it's a cultural identity — texting in full Arabic can feel overly formal among friends.
